title: Comparing Two Texts on the Same Topic

steps.instruction: Text A: 'Dolphins are playful ocean animals. They jump out of the water and swim in groups called pods. Dolphins are very smart and can learn tricks.' Text B: 'Dolphins face many dangers in the ocean. Fishing nets can trap them. Pollution makes the water dirty and can make dolphins sick.'
steps.work: Text A: Dolphins are playful, smart, swim in pods. Text B: Dolphins face dangers — nets, pollution.
steps.explanation: Both texts are about dolphins, but they focus on different things.

steps.instruction: What do both texts have in common?
steps.work: Same topic: dolphins. Same type: nonfiction/informational.
steps.explanation: Both give facts about dolphins in the ocean.

steps.instruction: How do the two texts differ in focus, tone, or purpose?
steps.work: Text A focuses on fun facts (positive). Text B focuses on problems dolphins face (serious/concerned).
steps.explanation: Text A wants you to appreciate dolphins. Text B wants you to understand their challenges. Two texts on the same topic can have very different purposes and tones.

steps.subgoalLabel: Explain what you learn from both
steps.instruction: What do you understand about dolphins by reading BOTH texts?
steps.work: Dolphins are smart, playful animals, but they face real dangers from humans. Reading both gives a more complete picture.
steps.explanation: Comparing two texts gives a fuller understanding than reading just one. Each text adds information the other leaves out.

title: Comparing How Two Authors Treat the Same Event

steps.instruction: Text A: 'The first day of school is exciting! You get to meet your new teacher and see your friends. There are new supplies and new things to learn.' Text B: 'The first day of school can be hard. Everything is new and unfamiliar. Some kids feel nervous about finding their classroom.'
steps.work: Text A: first day is exciting — friends, new supplies. Text B: first day can be hard — nervous, unfamiliar.
steps.explanation: Same event (first day of school), different perspectives.

steps.instruction: How does each author feel about the first day of school?
steps.work: Author A: positive, excited. Author B: understanding, acknowledges difficulty.
steps.explanation: Author A emphasizes the good parts. Author B emphasizes the challenging parts.

steps.instruction: Why might two authors write about the same topic so differently?
steps.work: They may have had different experiences, or they may be writing for different purposes. Author A might want to get kids excited; Author B might want to help nervous kids feel understood.
steps.explanation: Authors make choices about what to include and how to present it. Comparing texts helps readers see that there are multiple ways to think about the same topic.
